plan.masthead_subtitle: Heuristic macro view — LLM unavailable for week 2026-W21.

rates-curve.headline: The curve at 4.623000144958496 for the 10y
rates-curve.body_md: The yield curve steepened this week, widening the spread between short and longer-term borrowing costs. The three-month to 10-year spread reached 105.5 basis points, while the 10-year Treasury yield rose to 4.623%, reflecting market pricing on inflation and Federal Reserve policy expectations. A steeper curve typically signals investors are differentiating between near-term and longer-term risks. The stability of this configuration—and whether it persists as economic data arrives—will tell us much about confidence in the Fed's inflation-fighting efforts.

sectors-leader.headline: Energy leads the week
sectors-leader.body_md: Energy stocks dominated sector performance this week, posting a 5.96% five-day gain that significantly outpaced all major rivals. The rally follows a period of sector weakness and signals a shift in investor appetite toward oil and gas equities. The sharp move has drawn renewed trading attention to energy leadership. Traders are now watching to determine whether the momentum reflects underlying economic strength in energy demand or represents a temporary portfolio shift ahead of key economic data.

editorial-2026-W21.headline: Week ending 2026-05-18 — the macro picture
editorial-2026-W21.body_md: Markets sorted through competing signals on growth and inflation this week, grappling with what they mean for central bank policy ahead. Policymakers face a narrowing window to adjust course if their earlier projections no longer hold, while investors weigh how much economic slack remains. Bond and equity markets moved in lockstep around key data releases and Fed commentary, reflecting the high stakes of getting the macro call right. The coming weeks will test whether recent momentum can persist or whether headwinds prove more persistent. Inflation trends, employment strength, and any shift in central bank communication will be essential signals to watch.

sectors-leader.headline: Energy leads the week
sectors-leader.body_md:
Energy topped the S&P 500 sector scoreboard for the week ending May 18, posting a five-day gain of roughly 6% — a gap wide enough to make it the clear leader regardless of what the rest of the market did. And the rest of the market did not do particularly well: the Nasdaq and S&P 500 fell on Monday as the 10-year Treasury yield climbed to its highest level in more than a year, which made the energy sector's outperformance look even more pronounced.

The underlying story is not new, but it has not lost momentum. When U.S. forces struck Iran in late February, crude oil began a climb that took Brent futures from roughly $72 a barrel to a wartime peak near $120. The closure of the Strait of Hormuz — through which about 20% of global seaborne crude flows — removed a significant slice of supply from the market overnight. Since then, oil has settled into an elevated range: Brent was trading above $108 on May 18, and WTI futures were near $102. Those prices, sustained for three months, have flowed straight through to producer earnings.

## The week's specific catalyst — and the complication

For most of this week, the sector rode continued strength in crude, with WTI briefly touching $107 before retreating. But Friday brought a complication. Iran's semi-official Tasnim news agency reported that Washington had proposed a temporary waiver on Iranian oil sanctions as a goodwill measure to facilitate a final peace agreement. The U.S. did not confirm the offer. If such a waiver materialized and the Strait stayed open, Iranian barrels — largely absent from global markets since the war began — could gradually return, capping the geopolitical premium that has made energy the year's best-performing sector.

The market's reaction was instructive: oil pulled back several dollars on the report, and shares in Exxon Mobil, Chevron, and Occidental Petroleum dipped on Friday. Yet the five-day return for the sector still came in near 6%, which tells you how strong the first four days of the week were.

## Who benefited

Occidental Petroleum has been the closest thing to a pure oil-price play among the majors. The stock has risen more than 37% year-to-date and was up roughly 11% in just the past month heading into this week. In the first quarter, Occidental produced 1.4 million barrels of oil equivalent per day and delivered adjusted earnings per share of $1.06, well above analyst estimates.

Cheniere Energy and Cameco have both gained more than 24% in 2026. Cheniere, the dominant U.S. LNG exporter, reported first-quarter adjusted EBITDA of $2.3 billion — up 25% year-over-year — even as headline earnings were muddied by $5.4 billion in non-cash derivative losses. Natural gas prices have spiked alongside oil, and Cheniere's long-term contracted volumes have given it a relatively stable earnings floor even in a volatile environment.

The Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE), the most-watched proxy for the sector, attracted an estimated $6 billion in new inflows over the past three months as institutional and retail investors sought commodity exposure. XLE has broken above its 200-day moving average and was trading near $59.44 at the close on May 15, compared with a 52-week low of $40.36. The ETF is up roughly 39% year-to-date.

## The broader backdrop

The International Energy Agency has called the disruption caused by the 2026 Iran conflict "the greatest global energy security challenge in history" — a characterization that, however dramatic, reflects the arithmetic: the Strait of Hormuz closure at its peak removed more supply from global markets than any single event in recorded history. A two-week ceasefire announced by President Trump on April 7 reopened the strait temporarily, but formal negotiations over a lasting deal are still ongoing.

For investors, the question has never been whether energy companies benefit from high oil — they do, mechanically — but how long the premium holds. The sector has now run a 14-consecutive-week streak of gains within the S&P 500, the longest on record. That kind of streak invites skepticism.

The Minneapolis Federal Reserve noted in a recent paper that markets have been "looking through" the commodity shock, expecting a resolution that brings prices back toward pre-war levels. The European Central Bank has been less sanguine, warning that a prolonged conflict risks pushing Germany and Italy into technical recession by year-end as energy-intensive manufacturers absorb input costs they cannot fully pass on.

## What to watch

The Iran sanctions waiver story will be the clearest near-term signal. A confirmed U.S. offer — and Iranian acceptance — would mark a significant step toward normalizing supply, and the market would price that immediately. Conversely, a breakdown in negotiations, or any new disruption in the Strait of Hormuz, would likely push crude back toward its wartime highs and extend the sector's run.

Domestically, weekly U.S. crude inventory data remains a secondary but watched input: the American Petroleum Institute reported an 8.1-million-barrel draw for the week ending May 1, far larger than expected, which underscored how tight the domestic supply picture has become.

Q2 earnings season for the energy majors begins in late July. At current prices, the consensus expects another round of significant beats. Whether that matters to sector returns by then depends almost entirely on where oil is trading — and that, in turn, depends on whether ceasefire talks in the coming weeks produce anything durable.
sectors-leader.in_one_sentence: Energy led all S&P 500 sectors with a ~6% weekly gain, driven by elevated crude oil above $100 as Iran war ceasefire negotiations remain unresolved.
sectors-leader.image_caption: The Lun-A offshore gas platform near Sakhalin Island — one of thousands of production facilities whose output has gained outsized value since the 2026 Strait of Hormuz disruption tightened global supply.

rates-curve.headline: The curve at 4.623000144958496 for the 10y
rates-curve.body_md:
The 10-year Treasury note finished this week yielding 4.623%, a level last seen in January 2025. The 3-month bill sits near 3.57%, leaving the 3m10y spread — the measure economists most closely watch for recession signals — at positive 105.5 basis points. That number alone might look reassuring after years of inversion. The way the curve got here is anything but.

For roughly 27 months beginning in late 2022, the US yield curve was inverted: short-term borrowing cost more than long-term, the longest such inversion in modern US history. The inversion finally broke in April 2026. What followed was not the "bull steepening" that fixed-income managers had penciled into their year-ahead forecasts — the scenario where the Fed cuts rates, short yields drift lower, and the curve gradually normalizes. Instead, the curve has steepened from the wrong end.

This is a bear steepener. Long-dated yields are climbing faster than short-dated yields are falling. The 30-year Treasury crossed 5% this week for the first time since autumn 2023. The 2-year remains near 4.09%, pinned by a Federal Reserve that has kept its overnight target on hold all year. The long end is moving on its own logic — and that logic is inflation.

## The data that broke the week

Tuesday's Producer Price Index for April came in at +1.4% month-on-month, the largest monthly gain since March 2022. The Dow Jones consensus had expected +0.5%. The miss sent 10-year yields jumping roughly 10 basis points in a single session. Compounding the shock, oil prices moved higher amid renewed tensions in the Middle East, reinforcing fears of an energy-driven inflation pulse that central bank rate policy cannot easily address.

Combined, the two inputs — domestic PPI overshoot and an external energy supply threat — spent the back half of the week repricing not just the near-term inflation outlook but the structural term premium embedded in long-dated Treasuries. Term premium, the extra yield investors demand for lending at longer maturities beyond what rate expectations alone justify, had been compressed for years by quantitative easing and an orderly Fed tightening cycle. That compression is now reversing. Breakeven inflation rates reflect it: the 5-year breakeven has climbed to around 2.70%, and the 10-year breakeven to approximately 2.49% — both above levels where the Fed would typically feel comfortable declaring mission accomplished.

## A new chair, an old question

The backdrop to this week's moves is the confirmation of Kevin Warsh as the 17th Chair of the Federal Reserve, succeeding Jerome Powell. The Senate confirmed him in May. Markets have spent months debating what a Warsh Fed would mean in practice; early signals from bond traders suggest that uncertainty itself has a price, and it is being extracted from the long end.

Warsh built his reputation as an inflation hawk after the 2008 financial crisis, arguing publicly that the Fed moved too slowly to normalize. His nomination was initially read by some as a signal of aggressive balance-sheet reduction. Now, with April inflation data running well above target and the energy complex under renewed pressure, some trading desks are pricing a non-trivial probability of a rate *hike* by early 2027 — not a cut. Futures markets show below 1% odds of a June reduction and rising tail risk on the upside.

The Fed has held its overnight target steady throughout 2026. The hot PPI print gives Warsh's Fed little room to pivot dovish, even as softer growth indicators would normally argue for easing. His first post-confirmation press conference, expected at the June FOMC meeting, will be closely parsed for any signal on where the dual mandate now ranks its priorities.

## What the shape means

A positively-sloped curve at these levels is not automatically a sign of economic health. The composition matters. In a bull steepener, short rates fall because the Fed is easing, credit conditions loosen, and the re-steepening reflects improving growth expectations. In a bear steepener, long rates rise because inflation expectations and term premiums are elevated — and that tightens financial conditions even without any Fed action. Mortgage rates key off the 10-year, not the overnight rate. Corporate borrowing costs do too. The repricing at the long end is already working its way into the real economy.

The 30-year at 5.02% is particularly worth watching. Thirty-year Treasuries are the most sensitive to long-run inflation expectations and to fiscal sustainability concerns. The level has not been sustained above 5% for more than brief interludes since the pre-quantitative easing era. Whether this week represents a temporary spike driven by two bad inflation prints and a geopolitical risk premium, or the beginning of a structural reset, is the central question for the rates market heading into summer.

## What to watch next

The May CPI print, due in mid-June, will be the next decisive data point. Another upside surprise would likely push 10-year yields toward the 4.8% range and test the 30-year's 2023 peak near 5.18%. A soft number would offer temporary relief — but the structural pressures, elevated energy prices, a new Fed Chair still establishing his inflation-fighting credibility, and a federal deficit that shows no sign of narrowing — are unlikely to dissolve on a single month's data.

The curve at 4.62% for the 10-year is not alarming in isolation. At similar levels in 2023 and early 2024, markets eventually found a ceiling. The difference now is the driver: then, the Fed was actively tightening and the direction of travel was known. Today, the Fed is on hold, a new chair has yet to demonstrate his hand, and the inflationary pressure is arriving from the supply side where rate hikes offer only limited relief. That combination — sticky short rates, rising long rates, and an inflation source that monetary policy cannot fully address — is precisely what makes a bear steepener more corrosive than it first appears.
rates-curve.in_one_sentence: The 10-year at 4.62% and a 3m10y spread of 105 bps mark a bear steepener — not a healthy normalization — as hot PPI data and a new Fed chair push long yields higher.
rates-curve.image_caption: US Treasury yield curves for selected dates, illustrating the shift from inverted to positively-sloped configurations.

editorial-2026-W21.headline: Week ending 2026-05-18 — the macro picture
editorial-2026-W21.body_md:
The bond market said something important this week, and it said it in plain arithmetic. On Thursday, the Treasury auctioned $25 billion in 30-year bonds at a yield of 5% — the first time that threshold has been breached since 2007. Demand was weak. Three- and ten-year auctions earlier in the week drew similarly thin interest. By Friday, the 10-year yield had settled at 4.623%, its highest close in more than a year.

What the bond market is pricing is not a technical anomaly. It reflects a growing conviction among fixed-income investors that inflation is not going quietly back to 2%, and that the force keeping it elevated — the Iran conflict — looks more like a structural shock than a transient one.

## The oil shock that won't quit

The U.S.-Israel military operation against Iran began on February 28. Twelve weeks on, the economic damage is widening. Brent crude traded above $105 a barrel this week, up 44% since the conflict began. The Strait of Hormuz — through which roughly one-fifth of global seaborne crude passes — has been disrupted for months. The International Energy Agency has described it as the largest supply disruption in the history of the global oil market.

At the pump, Americans are paying an average of $4.50 per gallon for gasoline, up from $2.98 before the conflict — a 51-cent increase that arrived in fewer than three months. That gap functions as a tax on every household that commutes, ships goods, or heats with fuel oil. The headline CPI ran at 3.3% on the latest read; the PCE measure the Fed prefers came in at 3.5% year-over-year. Core inflation is lower — 2.6% on CPI, 3.2% on core PCE — but the direction is wrong. Some economists now project PCE could reach 4% by year-end, double the central bank's target. Higher diesel costs are working through transport and logistics, which means the energy shock is not staying in the energy line item.

## The Fed's impossible position

The Federal Reserve is caught. Markets price in no rate cuts until December at the earliest; most forecasters now expect only one reduction across all of 2026. The logic is straightforward if uncomfortable: cutting rates while energy-driven inflation is accelerating would be difficult to defend. Raising rates into an economy that grew at only 2.0% annualized in the first quarter — a rebound, but not a robust one — risks choking the recovery entirely. So the Fed waits.

One consequence is that the long end of the curve is doing the work instead. As one analyst put it last week, tracking the 30-year auction: "Long-end rates are now in control of monetary policy." At 5% on the 30-year, the government's borrowing costs compound a budget problem that was already serious — the federal government pays roughly $1 trillion annually in interest on its debt. Every basis point the bond market extracts is real money, and weak auction demand suggests investors expect more of it.

## A summit that satisfied no one

Mid-week brought the conclusion of the Trump-Xi summit in Beijing, which produced a flurry of announcements and immediate disagreement about what those announcements meant.

President Trump said flatly that tariffs were not discussed. China's Ministry of Commerce announced agreement in principle on mutual tariff reductions, suggesting the sides would consider lifting levies on roughly $30 billion in goods — less than 10% of bilateral trade volumes. Both statements cannot simultaneously be accurate. In between, there were real commitments: China agreed to purchase at least $17 billion annually in U.S. agricultural products through 2028, and confirmed a deal covering purchases of U.S. aircraft and ongoing supply of jet engines and components. Trade and investment councils are to be established.

Markets treated the summit with appropriate skepticism — the yuan moved modestly, soybean futures ticked up, nothing moved violently. The gap between summit optics and enforceable agreements is a pattern investors have learned. What the summit did accomplish was to rule out near-term escalation in trade policy, which in the current environment is not nothing. Whether the investment councils produce binding commitments or become another venue for managed tension will become clear over the coming months.

## Equities: buying the AI story

The S&P 500 opened Monday at 7,403 — fractionally negative after reaching record highs last week. The Dow rose 0.3%; the Nasdaq slipped 0.5% as long yields pushed higher. What has kept equities near records despite a 44% oil shock and the bond market sending distress signals is a familiar narrative: artificial intelligence, and specifically Nvidia.

Nvidia reports first-quarter fiscal 2027 earnings on Wednesday, May 20. The company guided for $78 billion in revenue; Wall Street has penciled in $79.2 billion, representing 79.5% growth year-on-year. The stock is up 20% in the past month alone — a move that reflects both genuine confidence in AI data-center demand and a degree of speculative momentum. If those numbers arrive as expected, the read-through to the broader tech complex is positive. If guidance disappoints or gross margins compress, the effect will ripple.

The underlying tension is real. Higher long yields raise the discount rate on future earnings, which compresses multiples on growth stocks. Energy-driven cost pressures eat into margins across every capital-intensive industry. The S&P 500 at 7,400 is not cheap by historical standards. The bond market and the equity market are telling different stories about the next twelve months; one of them will be revised.

Walmart reports Thursday. Consumer spending has held up, but sentiment surveys have been weakening. The company's commentary on basket composition, trade-down behavior, and tariff pass-through will be more useful than the headline revenue figure for understanding where the consumer actually stands.

## What to watch

The flash PMI estimates for May arrive this week and will give the first look at whether private-sector activity held up as energy costs deepened. The Philadelphia Fed Manufacturing Index and the final University of Michigan consumer sentiment reading fill out the data calendar. Sentiment already hit a historic low earlier in the spring; whether that reflects a one-time shock or persistent deterioration matters for the second-half consumption outlook.

On the geopolitical side, the Strait of Hormuz remains the variable that overrides everything else. Any credible signal of reopening — formal or informal — would unwind a portion of the energy premium embedded in inflation expectations and yields. Any escalation would compound it. Markets have been pricing a gradual de-escalation for several weeks. If that assumption is wrong, the bond market's verdict will get harsher before it improves.

Energy leads the sector scoreboard this week. That is less a bullish signal than a symptom of the problem the rest of the economy is trying to absorb.
editorial-2026-W21.in_one_sentence: An oil shock now twelve weeks old is cracking the bond market — the 30-year hit 5% for the first time since 2007 — while the Fed waits and equity markets watch Nvidia.

for_strategies_md:
```markdown
**Bias for the week ahead:**

- _Rates:_ Curve steep (1.055% 3m10y spread) signaling easing expectations — favor curve steepeners; monitor 10Y at 4.623% for technical breaks.
- _FX:_ Dollar extending (+1.04% 5d) — EM volatility rising; support holds 98-99; watch for reversal as carry unwinds accelerate.
- _Credit:_ Spreads widening (LQD -1.17%, HYG -0.55%) — risk-off tone despite oil rally; lower-rated credits under pressure.
- _Sectors:_ Energy (+5.96% 5d, +10% 20d) structurally supported by oil strength (+7.67%); tactical short on Discretionary (-2.56%), lean Staples. Tech weakness (5d: -1.98%) may be consolidation given 20d strength.
- _Regions:_ Dollar strength pressures EM; commodity exporters outperform developed.
- _Risk:_ Precious metals crash (Silver -10.33%) — reversal of safe-haven flows or commodity bubble unwind.

**Watchlist:** XLE, USO, XLP, GLD, LQD, HYG, XLK, XLY, DBC, DXY, EEM, TLT
```
